cards.back: Merge keeps both histories + a merge commit; rebase replays for a linear history (rewrites hashes).
soft keeps index+files; mixed resets index; hard discards file changes too.
Shelve uncommitted changes for a clean tree; "stash pop" brings them back.
HEAD points at a commit, not a branch; new commits can be lost. Make a branch.
fetch downloads remote changes; pull = fetch + merge into your branch.
Copy one specific commit onto your branch without merging the whole source.
Tells git which files never to track; already-tracked files aren't affected.
Replaces the last commit; avoid on already-pushed commits.
revert adds a new undo commit (safe for shared history); reset moves the pointer back.
A branch moves as you commit; a tag is a fixed label on one commit (a release).
Stages changes hunk by hunk, so you can split one file into focused commits.
The commit one before HEAD; HEAD~2 is two back — handy for reset/rebase.

cards.front: How to resolve a merge conflict?
cards.back: Edit the <<<< ==== >>>> markers to the desired result, then add and commit.
git log --oneline shows one line per commit — quick overview.

cards.front: See what you're about to commit?
cards.back: git diff --staged shows staged changes; git diff shows unstaged.
The default name for your main remote repo (e.g. on GitHub).
Sets the upstream so future "git push/pull" need no arguments.
Shows who last changed each line and in which commit — for tracing history.
Combining several commits into one (via rebase -i) for a cleaner history.
Clone copies a repo locally; fork makes your own server-side copy (e.g. on GitHub).
git restore <file> discards its unstaged changes (was git checkout -- <file>).

cards.front: What makes a good commit message?
cards.back: A short imperative summary ("Fix login crash"), details in the body if needed.
It can overwrite others' commits on a shared branch; prefer --force-with-lease.
